prompt: John Adams defends the Alien and Sedition Acts as necessary wartime measures to protect national security against French agents and dangerous political agitators. He argues the federal government must have power to expel dangerous foreigners and punish seditious libel. Mr. Madison, what is your response to President Adams's defense of these laws?
ground_truth_signal: Madison authored the Virginia Resolutions of 1798 explicitly condemning the Alien and Sedition Acts as unconstitutional usurpations. He argued the Sedition Act violated the First Amendment and that the federal government had no enumerated power over alien residence or press freedoms. Importantly, Madison's Virginia Resolutions used interposition — state authority to declare federal acts unconstitutional — as a check. He went further than mere political opposition to argue the acts were void as contrary to the constitutional compact.
response:
Mr. Adams's defense of these measures, however well-intentioned, rests upon premises that strike at the very foundation of our constitutional system. I cannot accept that the exigencies of our present difficulties with France justify such a departure from the principles that ought to govern a free republic.

First, let us examine the constitutional question. The Alien Act assumes a federal power to expel foreigners that exists nowhere in the enumerated authorities granted to Congress. The Constitution delegates to the federal government certain specified powers — to regulate naturalization, to define and punish piracies and felonies on the high seas, to declare war and raise armies. But where does it grant authority over the general conduct of aliens residing peacefully within our borders? Such a power, if it exists at all, would seem to fall within that vast reservoir of authority reserved to the states, who retain jurisdiction over the lives, liberties, and properties of persons within their territories.

The Sedition Act presents an even graver constitutional difficulty. Congress is expressly forbidden by the First Amendment from making any law abridging the freedom of speech or of the press. Yet this statute makes it a crime to publish false, scandalous, and malicious writings against the government, the Congress, or the President. How can a law that punishes speech be reconciled with a constitutional prohibition against laws abridging speech? The distinction between liberty and licentiousness, however appealing in theory, becomes in practice a license for magistrates to suppress whatever opinions they find inconvenient or dangerous.

Experience has shown us the tendency of such laws in the hands of those who wield power. In Britain, the doctrine of seditious libel has been the favorite weapon of ministers seeking to silence their critics. Every administration finds the writings of its opponents false, scandalous, and malicious. What security do we have that our own magistrates will prove more temperate than their British counterparts?

Mr. Adams contends that these measures are necessary to protect us from French agents and domestic conspirators. But consider the remedy he proposes. The Alien Act places the liberty of every foreign-born resident at the discretion of the President alone, without trial, without hearing, upon his mere suspicion of danger. This is the very species of arbitrary power against which we took up arms in our revolution. A government that can expel men without trial can imprison them without trial — and if it can imprison the foreign-born upon suspicion, what principle restrains it from imprisoning the native-born upon the same ground?

The Sedition Act, meanwhile, makes the federal judiciary the arbiter of political truth. Juries may determine whether particular statements are false, but who is to say what constitutes malicious intent? What standard will distinguish legitimate criticism from seditious libel? The law promises that truth shall be a defense, but this provides cold comfort when the government itself determines what truth is, and when the expense and uncertainty of a criminal trial must deter all but the most reckless from testing the boundaries of permitted speech.

I do not dispute that the nation faces genuine dangers. The conduct of the French Directory toward our commerce and our diplomatic representatives has been both insulting and injurious. There may indeed be French agents within our borders seeking to corrupt our citizens and undermine our government. But the question is not whether dangers exist — it is whether the proposed remedies are consistent with the nature of our constitutional system and whether they are likely to produce the security they promise.

A government that can silence its critics by prosecuting them for sedition has removed the most essential check upon its own misconduct. Public opinion, operating through a free press, is the only reliable guardian of public liberty in a republic. Destroy this guardian, and you leave the people defenseless against the encroachments of their rulers. The short-term security these laws may provide will be purchased at the cost of the long-term liberty they are meant to protect.

Moreover, these measures appear calculated to serve partisan rather than national purposes. The Alien Act targets primarily those immigrants who have shown sympathy for the Republican cause, while the Sedition Act has been employed almost exclusively against Republican editors and writers. This suggests that the true object is not the protection of national security but the suppression of political opposition. Such a use of federal power transforms the government from an impartial guardian of the public good into an engine of faction — precisely the evil our constitutional system was designed to prevent.

The proper remedy for dangerous speech is not suppression but more speech. The proper security against foreign influence is not arbitrary deportation but the vigilance of an informed citizenry. If French agents are indeed corrupting our people, let their activities be exposed by investigation and debate, not concealed by the secrecy that arbitrary power requires. If seditious writings threaten the public peace, let them be answered by better arguments, not silenced by criminal prosecutions.

I fear that Mr. Adams, in his zeal to protect the nation from external enemies, has inadvertently provided those enemies with their greatest victory. What conquest could serve French interests better than to see America abandon the very principles that distinguish her from the despotic governments of Europe? The Alien and Sedition Acts do not make us more secure — they make us more like those from whom we seek security.
